description: This test case evaluates the RAG system's robustness in retrieving a specific, rare, or hard-to-find fact (the 'needle') from a large context ('haystack').
question: Explain the underlying physical mechanisms and defect types responsible for Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) in CMOS technology as described by the As-grown-Generation (AG) model, including how these defects contribute differently to BTI kinetics and recovery.

gold_context_sources.evidence: The as-grown-generation (AG) model was presented, which can predict the long-term BTI under operation biases. An in-depth understanding of underlying physical processes led to the proposal of a framework for the defects responsible for BTI. There are as-grown defects in fresh devices before electrical stresses, which are deﬁned as the traps whose ﬁrst charging is the same as any subsequent charging after neutralization. Their density and properties will not be changed by stresses. They can be further divided into as-grown hole/electron traps (AHTs/AETs) and energy-alternating defects (EADs). The AHTs/AETs are ﬁlled rapidly and can dominate the initial BTI. They are saturated with stress time and are responsible for the “hump” often observed in the BTI kinetics. They can be readily neutralized and make a major contribution to the recovery of BTI. Their energy levels do not change after charging. In contrast, the energy level of an EAD becomes deeper after charging, making them more stable than AHTs/AETs. Their charging follows a power law with a power exponent generally different from that of defect generation. In addition to the as-grown defects, new defects can be generated by converting precursors to electrically active traps. This conversion process may only happen once and is slow and rate-limiting. Filling the generated traps is relatively fast, and the generated traps will not return to their precursor status under normal BTI stress conditions. Some of the generated traps can be repeatedly charged–discharged by alternating gate bias polarity, and they are called cyclic positive charges/electron traps. The rest of generated traps can survive the recovery and are referred to as anti-neutralization positive charges/electron traps.

ground_truth_answer: The As-grown-Generation (AG) model explains Bias Temperature Instability (BTI) in CMOS technology by identifying specific defect types and their physical mechanisms. BTI arises from defects present in fresh devices before electrical stress, called as-grown defects, whose first charging behavior is consistent with subsequent chargings after neutralization, and their properties remain unchanged by stress. These defects are categorized into as-grown hole traps (AHTs), as-grown electron traps (AETs), and energy-alternating defects (EADs). The AHTs and AETs fill rapidly and dominate the initial BTI response, saturating with stress time and causing the characteristic 'hump' in BTI kinetics. They can be readily neutralized, contributing significantly to BTI recovery, and their energy levels remain constant after charging. Conversely, EADs deepen their energy levels upon charging, making them more stable and less readily neutralized than AHTs/AETs. Their charging kinetics follow a power law with an exponent distinct from defect generation. Beyond as-grown defects, new defects can be generated by converting precursors into electrically active traps through a slow, rate-limiting, one-time process. These generated traps fill quickly but do not revert to precursors under normal BTI stress. Some generated traps exhibit cyclic charging behavior under alternating gate bias polarity (cyclic positive charge/electron traps), while others persist through recovery (anti-neutralization positive charge/electron traps). This defect framework underpins the AG model's ability to predict long-term BTI behavior under operating biases, distinguishing contributions of different defect types to BTI kinetics and recovery phenomena.
